papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper studies how a cloud of ultralight scalar dark matter around a massive black hole changes the gravitational wave signals produced when a small compact object spirals inward — a system called an extreme-mass-ratio inspiral (EMRI), a key target for the planned LISA space detector. The paper argues that a previously uncomputed gravitational effect — the "polar" metric sector coupling to the scalar cloud — can actually dominate all other dark-matter-induced corrections to the waves in parts of the relevant parameter range. The review board found this a timely and credible gap-filling contribution, though the results are demonstrated for an idealized spherical, non-spinning setup with an optimistic cloud mass, and the detectability implications for realistic superradiant clouds around spinning black holes require further work.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper presents the first fully coupled computation of the polar gravitational-wave flux sector for circular EMRIs in a spherically symmetric scalar cloud on a Schwarzschild background, extending the Zerilli formalism to a six-dimensional coupled scalar-metric perturbation system. The paper claims that polar metric backreaction corrections can dominate axial and scalar dissipative channels for scalar field masses μM ≲ 0.2 at small separations, and that conservative geodesic corrections from cloud backreaction on the metric can further dominate total waveform dephasing. Two analytic limits are presented: a gravitational redshift at small separation/low cloud compactness, and a mass shift at large separation/high compactness. Numerical validation uses the Black Hole Perturbation Toolkit for vacuum benchmarks and reproduces prior scalar flux results. An extrapolation procedure removes unphysical O(α²) terms arising from the perturbative expansion in scalar field amplitude.
papers.title: Relativistic signatures of scalar dark matter in extreme-mass-ratio inspirals

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper introduces GreyRing, a new mathematical tool for analyzing the dying "ringdown" signal from two merged black holes. Instead of the standard technique of decomposing the signal into discrete vibration modes, GreyRing uses a quantity called the greybody factor — which encodes how the merged black hole's curved spacetime scatters gravitational waves across a broad range of frequencies. The review board found the approach technically promising and impressively accurate in matching simulated waveforms. The main caveat is that the paper's bolder claims — especially that the method yields tighter measurements of the final black hole's mass and spin than established techniques — rest on an analysis where several sources of observational uncertainty are held fixed rather than properly propagated, making the precision comparison unreliable as stated.
papers.technical_takeaway: GreyRing models the frequency-domain post-merger gravitational-wave signal using the Kerr complex reflection amplitude R(ω) multiplied by an empirical amplitude correction and a phenomenological phase correction δφ(ω) = φ₁/ω (justified post-hoc as a Laurent expansion). The model achieves typical mismatches ~10^-4 for the dominant (2,2) mode across comparable-mass, aligned-spin SXS catalog simulations, claiming an order-of-magnitude improvement over TEOBPM. The paper proposes a GR consistency test comparing (M_f, χ_f) inferred from GreyRing with standard QNM spectroscopy, applied to GW250114. Three cross-cycle reviewers converged on: (1) the mismatch validation result is the paper's best-supported claim within the tested nonprecessing/quasicircular regime; (2) the "slightly narrower posteriors" precision claim for GW250114 is not like-for-like because sky location, distance, inclination, and polarization are fixed rather than marginalized; (3) the lower frequency cutoff f_low introduces a systematic analogous to the time-domain start-time choice the method claims to avoid; (4) the phase model lacks a first-principles derivation; and (5) the core theoretical grounding rests on a companion preprint (Ref. [111]) submitted by largely the same author group one month prior, not yet independently peer-reviewed.
papers.title: Novel ringdown tests of general relativity with black hole greybody factors

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper asks whether extremal black holes — those at the boundary between "charged enough to have one horizon" and "too charged to exist" — can be genuinely stable, or whether they always suffer a slow-burning mathematical instability called the Aretakis effect. The authors show that the worse the instability is for ordinary extremal black holes, the more it can be tamed by making the horizon "more degenerate" (loosely, by forcing more and more derivatives of the metric to vanish there). They then propose a hypothetical geometry with an infinitely degenerate horizon that appears to be free of this instability entirely, offering a candidate for a stable resting state of black hole evolution. The review board found the finite-degeneracy analysis clean and well-supported, but the infinitely-degenerate stability claim rests on an assumption — that the field itself stays bounded — that is only verified numerically for one specific example, not proved analytically.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper argues that for a static spherically symmetric black hole with a horizon of degeneracy rank k (meaning f and its first k−1 radial derivatives vanish at the horizon), the Aretakis instability for massless minimally coupled scalar probe fields persists but is progressively screened: the first k transverse horizon derivatives of the s-wave mode approach constants at late times, and the (k+1)-th derivative blows up as t^1, with the n-th derivative (n > k) growing as t^{n−k}. This is demonstrated analytically via a near-horizon Aretakis-constant recursion (Appendix A) and via a Lifshitz near-horizon throat scaling argument (Section 3.3), and corroborated numerically for k = 1, 2, 3 and an infinitely-degenerate example. For k ≥ 2, only the l = 0 s-wave mode survives in the leading near-horizon throat limit. For an infinitely-degenerate horizon (f^{(n)}(r_H) = 0 for all n), infinitely many s-wave Aretakis constants prevent any finite-order transverse derivative from blowing up, conditional on the field itself remaining bounded — a premise that is established numerically for one metric (f ~ exp(−1/r²)) but not proved generally. The stability argument is further restricted to massless scalars; the massive scalar case, which lacks locally defined Aretakis constants but still exhibits instability on standard extremal horizons, is not addressed.
papers.title: Taming the Aretakis instability: extremal black holes with multi-degenerate horizons

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper by leading mathematical relativists attempts to prove that a suitably weighted "holographic energy" — an energy concept arising in the AdS/CFT correspondence of string theory — is always non-negative for a broad class of four-dimensional spacetimes with a negative cosmological constant. The review board found the core idea genuinely novel: a conformal weighting factor derived from two-dimensional geometry on the boundary elegantly extends positivity beyond previously known cases. The proof is presented as a compact 7-page letter and, while the overall framework is considered plausible and likely correct, three computational steps central to the argument are asserted rather than demonstrated, most importantly the cancellation of a divergent term at the heart of the main result and the treatment of black hole inner boundaries.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper adapts the Witten spinor argument (via the Schrödinger-Lichnerowicz-Sen-Witten identity) to prove positivity of the conformally weighted holographic charge (II.40) for four-dimensional ALAdS spacetimes with conformally static conformal boundary and spherical or trivial-spin toroidal cross-sections. The central novelty is identifying the conformal weight φ with uniformization data from solutions of the 2D twistor equation on boundary sections, which extends positivity beyond the Birmingham-Kottler/conformally-Einstein case. Three reviewers across two cycles converge on the following assessment: the proof outline is plausible and the result likely correct given the authors' expertise, but three steps are asserted rather than demonstrated — (1) the cancellation of the apparent O(ρ⁻¹) divergent boundary term between (II.38) and (II.40) via an unshown "commutation calculation"; (2) the handling of inner SLSW boundary contributions at outer/marginally-outer-trapped surfaces; and (3) the L² membership of the right-hand side of (II.34) needed to invoke the Witten spinor existence theorem. The Siklos-wave rigidity at equality is conditional on a Cauchy development existing, and higher-dimensional extensions are sketched rather than proved.
papers.title: Positivity of holographic energy

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper presents the most comprehensive simulations yet of spinning black holes surrounded by massive, magnetically threaded accretion disks that are tilted relative to the black hole spin axis. The review board found the work genuinely novel: the central finding—that magnetic turbulence can amplify rather than suppress disk instabilities when the black hole spin points opposite to the disk rotation—is physically non-obvious. The main caveat is that several standard quality checks for this class of simulation (demonstrating that the magnetic turbulence is physically resolved rather than numerical, and at least one convergence test) are not shown in the paper, and multiple presentation errors (a misattributed key citation, wrong figure cross-references) need correction before the quantitative claims can be trusted at face value.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper claims the first fully self-consistent GRMHD treatment of tilted, self-gravitating BHD systems in a dynamical spacetime. The central result is a tilt-dependent MRI–PPI coupling: the m=1 Papaloizou–Pringle mode is magnetically damped in the aligned configuration (A1) but is amplified by MRI-driven turbulence in the antialigned configuration (A4), yielding enhanced GW emission and rapid BH spin-down in A4. The proposed mechanism is that the MRI growth timescale is much shorter than the hydrodynamic PPI timescale, so MRI creates an effectively viscous background first; in misaligned systems this background accelerates angular momentum transport and nonlinear PPI growth, while in aligned systems it produces net damping. Key technical gaps identified unanimously across both review cycles include: absent MRI quality factor (Q_z, Q_phi) diagnostics, no numerical convergence study for the reported growth rates, unconfirmed SANE vs. MAD classification (no phi_BH time series), and a misattributed BZ mechanism citation (Blandford & Payne 1982 cited instead of Blandford & Znajek 1977, with BZ 1977 absent from the reference list entirely). The causal MRI–PPI interpretation rests on indirect evidence (faster accretion, higher mode amplitudes, density compression) rather than direct stress tensor or angular momentum flux diagnostics.
papers.title: Multimessenger Signatures of Tilted, Self-Gravitating, Black Hole Disks

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper tries to build a mathematical model for the gravitational-wave signal emitted just after two black holes on highly elongated (eccentric) orbits collide and merge — a scenario that standard gravitational-wave models handle poorly. The review board found the work promising: the new template improves on existing models by roughly an order of magnitude for the most eccentric cases, and the approach is practically useful for future gravitational-wave analyses. The main caveat is that the model's accuracy has only been tested on the same simulations used to build it, so how well it generalises to new cases remains unconfirmed.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper extends the RatExp post-merger amplitude ansatz (originally from Ref. [21] for extreme-mass-ratio inspirals) to the comparable-mass case for non-spinning binaries in highly eccentric orbits. Bayesian nested sampling extracts local QNM amplitude and phase coefficients from 233 single-resolution RIT simulations; a multivariate polynomial global fit in symmetric mass ratio η and dynamics-based merger parameters (effective energy Ê_eff, angular momentum ĵ_eff) is constructed. The best three-parameter cubic fit achieves mismatches within ~10⁻³–10⁻² on the calibration set, which is the paper's headline result. However, the global fits are evaluated in-sample, the bimodal posterior branch selection is not statistically justified, the merger-time inflection-point filter is unspecified, and external validation against the SXS catalog is limited to a single low-eccentricity case despite stronger abstract claims.
papers.title: Highly eccentric non-spinning binary black hole mergers: quadrupolar post-merger waveforms

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper uses a theoretical framework called holographic duality to model how a strongly-coupled quantum fluid changes phase—much like water boiling—under unusual parameter settings. The review board found the central observation plausibly novel: a single coupling parameter can push the system through not one but two phase-transition critical points, producing a "re-entrant" phase structure. The main caveat is that this result is demonstrated purely numerically on a narrow slice of parameter space, with no analytical explanation for why a second critical point must exist, and with key numerical documentation missing.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper claims a "double critical phenomenon" in a probe-limit AdS₄ holographic superfluid whose Lagrangian includes λ₄|ψ|⁴ and λ₆|ψ|⁶ scalar self-interactions and a Maxwell-sector non-minimal coupling h(ψ)=exp(α|ψ|²)F²: as α increases with λ₄ and λ₆ fixed, the spinodal region of first-order phase transitions first collapses to a critical point, enters a supercritical phase, and then reconstitutes at a second critical point. Free-energy and quasinormal mode (QNM) stability analyses are reported to agree across second-order, zeroth-order, and Cave-of-Wind (COW) transition examples. Reviewers in cycle 2 reached consensus that the coupling is h(ψ)F², not an ηR|ψ|² curvature coupling, so the probe-limit mass-shift objection raised in cycle 1 was retracted. Persistent gaps: the canonical-ensemble free energy is asserted without deriving the Legendre transform and boundary terms; critical endpoints are identified visually rather than by stated numerical criteria; QNM spectral convergence is undocumented; and the double-critical region is mapped only on a one-dimensional parameter slice.
papers.title: Stability analysis and double critical phenomenon in the Einstein-Maxwell-scalar theory

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper asks whether ignoring a specific orbital phenomenon — transient resonances, which occur when an inspiralling object's orbital frequencies reach a simple ratio — would cause scientists to misread the properties of gravitational-wave sources in future space-based detectors like LISA. The review board finds the qualitative warning credible: the paper presents consistent evidence that these resonances leave a detectable imprint, and that modeling their sign and amplitude correctly is important. However, two structural limitations of the analysis mean the specific numerical bias estimates should not be taken at face value, and the results should be read as a well-motivated case study rather than a definitive quantitative forecast.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper applies a Fisher-matrix analysis — using numerical kludge (NK) waveforms augmented by a phenomenological effective resonance model (ERM) with orbit-dependent Teukolsky-based coefficients — to estimate systematic parameter bias when resonance crossings are omitted from template waveforms. Two objections survived all review cycles: (1) the Cutler-Vallisneri first-order bias formula is applied in large-mismatch regimes (mismatches up to 1.030) where the authors themselves acknowledge it is invalid, so tabulated bias ratios in those cases are qualitative indicators only; and (2) the Fisher parameter space excludes extrinsic parameters under an unvalidated degeneracy assumption, which likely deflates statistical error bounds and inflates the bias-to-uncertainty ratios. The strongest internally-consistent finding is that sign and amplitude of resonance-induced modifications to the constants of motion are decisive, with sign changes moving weaker-resonance biases across the significance threshold — a result that does not depend on the large-mismatch regime.
papers.title: Parameter-estimation bias induced by transient orbital resonances in extreme-mass-ratio inspirals

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This 7-page letter tries to give a single recipe for computing the thermodynamics — temperature, entropy, and energy — of a broad class of black holes that appear in quantum-gravity phenomenology, including so-called "regular" black holes that avoid a central singularity. The review board found the conceptual strategy promising and the worked example consistent, but identified two technical gaps — concerning whether the entropy formula is uniquely pinned down and whether a key dimensional-reduction step is fully justified — that are not resolved in the letter itself. A further caveat is that the foundational theorems the letter builds on are stated in concurrent companion preprints rather than proved here.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper argues that any D-dimensional static black hole satisfying g_tt g_rr = -1 in Schwarzschild gauge can be interpreted as a vacuum solution of an integrable 2D dilaton/Horndeski theory, with a generating function Ω encoding both the metric and the thermodynamics. The mass is identified as M = Ω(r_+) at the outer horizon, the Hawking temperature follows from differentiating the level curve Ω(r, f) = M, and the Wald entropy is claimed to equal S = −4π ∫ dr_+ ∂_f Ω evaluated at f = 0, derived by reducing the D-dimensional Noether charge to a partial derivative of the 2D Lagrangian. A generalized first law and Smarr formula follow for generating functions that are homogeneous in the relevant dimensionful scales. Two objections survived adversarial cross-review as potentially verdict-changing: (1) the entropy formula depends on an integration constant in the definition of the 2D Lagrangian coefficient h_4 (eq. 34), and different constants — corresponding to topological or boundary terms — can shift the Wald entropy without altering the field equations, so the formula is not uniquely fixed by the generating function Ω alone; (2) the reduction of the full D-dimensional Iyer-Wald entropy tensor to a partial derivative of the 2D reduced Lagrangian is asserted but not derived for the broad class of non-polynomial or curvature-derivative parent theories advertised. The reconstruction theorem, Birkhoff theorem, and integrability condition that underpin the framework are deferred to concurrent companion preprints.
papers.title: $g_{tt}g_{rr} =-1$ black hole thermodynamics in extended quasi-topological gravity

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proves that for any nonlinear electromagnetic theory that reduces to standard Maxwell electrodynamics at low field strength, the special case of equal electric and magnetic charges always contains a solution where the field behaves exactly like ordinary Maxwell electromagnetism. The review board found the core mathematical argument to be technically sound and consistently supported across six specific worked examples. The result is a useful organizing principle for a specialized community but applies only to the narrow equal-charge case and produces spacetime geometries already known from standard Maxwell theory. The main caveat is that several extensions claimed in the paper — to scalar-tensor gravity across conformal frames and to charged matter sources — are asserted without full proof.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper claims that for any NED Lagrangian L(F) satisfying a correct Maxwell limit (L_F(0)=1), the dyonic field equation with equal-magnitude charges q_e²=q_m² always admits F=0 as an algebraic root, whereupon the NED stress-energy tensor reduces to the Maxwell one (given the implicit additional condition L(0)=0) and the metric in GR is the dyonic Reissner-Nordström-de Sitter solution. The core algebraic substitution was verified at high reviewer confidence from the PDF. Gaps surviving both review cycles include: L(0)=0 left implicit rather than stated; the STT bi-frame claim is asserted without proof despite NED's conformal non-invariance; the algebraic existence is pointwise and the global claim "F=0 in the whole space" requires branch-consistency across the manifold; physical stability of the F=0 branch against perturbations is unaddressed; and the extension to charged matter uses source-free equations without deriving the sourced case.
papers.title: On gravitating dyonic configurations in nonlinear electrodynamics

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes a new organizational perspective on Birkhoff's theorem — the century-old result in general relativity that any spherically symmetric vacuum spacetime must be the Schwarzschild black hole geometry. The review board found the core mathematical argument to be locally sound, but concluded the novelty is modest: it repackages classical derivations in new "optical seed" language rather than proving a new rigidity result. The most important caveat is that the paper's converse theorem depends heavily on an unreviewed companion paper by the same group, and that converse already assumes stationarity rather than deriving it, making it weaker than the original Birkhoff theorem.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper constructs normalized 1-forms α = dr/φ and β = ∗dr/φ on the 2D orbit space of a spherically symmetric vacuum spacetime, where φ = 1 − 2m/r (the Misner-Sharp constant-energy condition). It argues these forms are closed and their null combinations ω± = α ± β are exact, integrating to local Eddington-Finkelstein coordinates with the metric in Kerr-Schild form — without first passing through static coordinates. The forward argument (Sections II–V) is independently confirmed algebraically by a reviewer with full PDF access. The stationary optical converse (Section VI, Theorem 9) — that spherical symmetry forces the inverse optical seed to ±1/r and reconstructs Schwarzschild — is conditional on arXiv:2604.05103 (Easson-Falato, April 2026), a concurrent unreviewed companion paper whose reconstruction hypotheses are not restated here. Additionally, Theorem 9 operates within an already-stationary framework, so it classifies spherically symmetric solutions within the stationary Kerr-Schild class rather than deriving stationarity from scratch, making it a weaker statement than canonical Birkhoff rigidity.
papers.title: Birkhoff rigidity from a covariant optical seed

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper is a short technical reply defending a 2015 result about quantum fields near an accelerating cavity. A critic argued the original calculation was methodologically flawed because it used mathematical tools associated with a region of spacetime causally disconnected from the cavity. The authors show the same result can be derived without those tools, and explain why using them was harmless anyway. Reviewers found the defense technically sound for its narrow purpose, but routine in contribution and limited in broader significance.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper aims to re-derive the O(λ²) cavity de-excitation probability of Lorek-Louko-Dragan (2015) via first-order perturbation theory restricted entirely to the right Rindler wedge, with the ambient field initialized in the Unruh thermal state (the Minkowski vacuum reduced to the right wedge). The derivation exploits global hyperbolicity of the right Rindler wedge with its constant-η Cauchy foliation. The causality argument asserts that since the interaction Hamiltonian is supported only within the cavity, physical transition probabilities are independent of the spatial extent of the basis modes used in intermediate steps — by analogy with inertial cavities where Minkowski plane waves extend over all of spacetime without violating causality. The paper claims Eq. (11) agrees with Eq. (19) of the 2015 paper; this equality is structurally plausible but cannot be independently verified from the reply alone, as the explicit mode functions are deferred to the original paper.
papers.title: Reply to 'Comment on "Ideal clocks -- a convenient fiction'' '

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper investigates a gravitational wave event called GW231123 that appeared, in earlier analyses, to show tentative evidence for a massive graviton — a potential departure from Einstein's general relativity. The authors argue this is a measurement artifact: the event is likely gravitationally lensed (its signal bent around an intervening mass), and analyzing it without accounting for lensing causes the fitting algorithm to misinterpret the lensing distortion as a sign of modified gravity. The review board found the qualitative argument and multi-waveform consistency checks compelling, but raised concerns about whether the key statistical evidence (the Bayes factor favoring lensing) was fully auditable from the reported methods, and whether the single injection test was truly independent of the hypothesis it was meant to support.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper claims that unmodeled point-mass wave-optics lensing can be absorbed into a massive-graviton dispersion parameter during Bayesian parameter estimation of GW231123, producing a spurious nonzero graviton-mass posterior. Key reported evidence: (1) log₁₀ B = 3.6 favoring the lensed over unlensed IMRPhenomXPHM hypothesis (both retaining the graviton-mass parameter); (2) mutual convergence of IMRPhenomXPHM, IMRPhenomXO4a, and NRSur7dq4 posteriors to m_g ≈ 0 once lensing is included; (3) a single GW231123-conditioned lensed NRSur7dq4 injection with m_g=0, recovered with unlensed IMRPhenomXPHM, reproduces the observed spurious posterior; (4) a 90% credible upper bound m_g ≤ 1.8 × 10⁻²³ eV/c² under the lensed analysis. The multi-waveform convergence is the most persuasive piece of evidence. The Bayes factor rests on adaptive prior bounds that are not fully tabulated, the injection is event-conditioned and single-point, and no competing systematic models (eccentricity, calibration, non-Gaussian noise, extended lenses) are compared via Bayes factors.
papers.title: GW231123: False Massive Graviton Signatures from Unmodeled Point-Mass Lensing

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This three-page note argues that a certain type of mathematical constraint — called "second-class constraints" — cannot spoil the process of choosing a gauge in theories of physics like general relativity, using a compact matrix algebra argument. The review board found the core algebraic claim likely correct but raised a significant concern: the paper's most important conclusion, about modified theories of gravity, goes beyond what the proof actually establishes, and the paper's own discussion section acknowledges this gap while drawing the stronger conclusion anyway in an earlier section. The paper also discloses that it was prepared with the assistance of an AI model (Claude Opus 4.6).
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper claims to prove, via the Schur complement, that the determinant of the combined constraint-and-gauge-fixing matrix factorises as ±(det Δ)² det C, where Δ is the first-class gauge-fixing block and C is the second-class block. This algebraic identity, if correct, shows that Hamiltonian gauge-fixing admissibility is strictly independent of the second-class sector. In the algebraic case (gauge parameters entering without time derivatives), the paper aims to show this coincides with the Lagrangian completeness criterion of Motohashi, Suyama, and Takahashi (MST). However, the main application — concluding that Lagrangian completeness in modified gravity is unaffected by second-class constraints — requires this equivalence to extend to the non-algebraic regime. The paper itself states in Section V that such an extension "remains open," creating an internal conflict with the conclusion drawn in Section IV. A secondary concern is that the proof is a finite-dimensional matrix argument applied without functional-analytic justification to field-theoretic (distributional/operator-valued) constraint algebras.
papers.title: A note on complete gauge-fixing and the constraint algebra

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper tests whether gravitational waves from merging black holes show any deviation from what Einstein's general relativity predicts—specifically, whether the black holes are described by the Kerr solution. Using 11 new events from the latest LIGO-Virgo catalog (GWTC-4), the paper finds no such deviation and reports tighter constraints than prior analyses. The review board found the results scientifically plausible and useful as a catalog update, but the work reuses an existing framework without new methodology. The most important caveat is that the paper does not present a combined constraint from all 11 events together, which would be the strongest statement the dataset could support.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper applies the Johannsen-metric ppE framework from Das et al. (2024) to 11 GWTC-4 BBH inspiral events (M < 80 M⊙, SNR > 10), constraining deformation parameters α₁₃ and ε₃ one at a time via Bayesian inference (Bilby/Dynesty) with IMRPhenomXPHM as the base waveform. Individual posteriors are consistent with zero. Three moderate-severity concerns survived both review cycles: the assumption that dissipative-sector phase contributions are GR-equivalent is asserted but not derived for the Johannsen metric; the ppE correction is incorporated into a full IMR waveform without specifying frequency cutoffs or tapering; and no combined multi-event constraint is presented despite the paper treating the deformation parameters as universal. A major objection—how two individual compact-object deformations map to one event-level parameter in a BBH—was raised in cycle 2 and not rebutted in the manuscript. Cycle-1 concerns about missing equations and table values were resolved as HTML rendering artifacts in cycle 2.
papers.title: Improved Constraints on Non-Kerr Deviations from Binary Black Hole Inspirals Using GWTC-4 Data

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This 8-page conference proceedings paper reviews the Borde-Guth-Vilenkin (BGV) theorem — a well-known result saying any universe with net positive expansion cannot be extended infinitely into the past — and applies it to a modern cyclic cosmology proposed by Ijjas and Steinhardt. The review board found the paper's central physical conclusion (the Ijjas-Steinhardt model lacks an infinite past history) to be very likely correct, supported by solid prior published work. The most important caveat is that the paper's attempt to "generalize" the BGV theorem to arbitrary spacetimes actually introduces new restrictions not present in the original theorem, and a separate claim that BGV implicitly requires an energy condition is incorrect. As a conference proceedings contribution, these are significant but not fatal issues.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper argues that FRW spacetimes with positive average Hubble parameter H_av are geodesically past-incomplete (standard BGV), that the Ijjas-Steinhardt entropy-diluting cyclic model satisfies this criterion (net positive H_av across cycles implies incompleteness), and that BGV generalizes to non-FRW spacetimes via a zero-shear/vorticity geodesic congruence formulation. All three reviewers agree the IS model conclusion is physically correct. Three objections survived adversarial cross-review: (1) the paper incorrectly asserts BGV implicitly requires the Null Energy Condition — the original theorem is purely kinematic, requiring only H_av > 0; the loitering universe evades BGV because H_av → 0, not due to NEC violation; (2) the Section 3 "generalization" assumes a geodesic congruence with zero shear and vorticity, a condition unavailable in generic inhomogeneous spacetimes, making it more restrictive than the original 2003 BGV result rather than a genuine extension; (3) the Section 1 inference from a finite accumulated ∫H dτ to finite past proper time is logically incomplete without an explicit positive lower bound on H_av. A fourth disputed issue — whether the cyclic-model proper time integral uses an ill-defined dN/H substitution when H crosses zero — remains unresolved pending PDF inspection.
papers.title: Geodesic Completeness in General Cosmological Scenarios

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper argues that certain exotic black holes (with both electric and magnetic charges) can trap orbiting objects in multiple distinct "pockets" of gravity, unlike ordinary black holes which have only one. Two orbits that look topologically the same by one standard classification can still radiate gravitational waves quite differently because they sit in different pockets. The review board found the central idea interesting but identified a likely labeling error in the waveform descriptions and an incomplete proof that the orbits are truly in the same topological class — both issues require correction before the main claim is fully substantiated.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper aims to show that periodic orbits in a dyonic black hole spacetime (quasi-topological nonlinear electrodynamics) can share the same rational rotation number q̃ and winding number w — and thus nominally the same Levin-Perez-Giz topological class — yet produce gravitational waveforms with distinct amplitude modulations and harmonic spectra under the kludge quadrupole approximation. The review board identified two critical issues that survived adversarial cross-review: (1) the Levin-Perez-Giz class is fully specified by the triple (z, w, v), not q̃ alone, and the paper never verifies that z and v match across branches — Fig. 3 even notes "distinct zoom-whirl morphologies," which is inconsistent with the claimed topological equivalence; (2) the main text and appendix give inverted descriptions of which branch is burst-like versus smooth, with physical reasoning favoring the appendix account (a tightly confined deep-well orbit is more circular, not burst-like). The waveform calculations rely on a kludge approximation that the authors themselves acknowledge is invalid in the deep strong-field regime used for the inner branch. Detector accessibility is claimed from sensitivity-curve overlap without any SNR estimate.
papers.title: Topologically equivalent yet radiatively distinct orbits in EMRI system

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes that a quantum two-level system (an "atom") undergoing uniform acceleration inside a carefully designed cylindrical cavity can maintain its quantum coherence better than the same atom sitting still — the opposite of what the standard Unruh effect would predict. The review board found the central physical mechanism plausible and grounded in established mathematics, but identified three technical concerns that limit confidence in the strongest claims: the paper overstates "exact" vanishing of decoherence when only strong suppression is demonstrated, applies a standard approximation (Markovian dynamics) in regimes where it formally breaks down, and makes an incomplete argument for a secondary thermalization claim. The core counter-intuitive effect is likely real, but several quantitative conclusions need more careful mathematical justification.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper argues that the decoherence rate of an Unruh-DeWitt detector in a cylindrical cavity equals one-half the emission rate in both inertial and accelerated regimes, making it proportional to the Purcell enhancement factor. The physical suppression mechanism — oscillatory zeros of Macdonald functions K_{iΩ/a}(m_n/a) governing the accelerated detector's response to transverse cavity modes — was confirmed by expert reviewers as physically grounded. Three objections survived cross-review: (1) the paper's claim that rates "exactly vanish" for finely tuned cavity parameters is unsupported, because tuning a single geometric parameter (cavity radius) cannot simultaneously extinguish contributions from the infinite sum of transverse modes; (2) the Markov master equation is formally invalid at cavity resonances where the spectral density diverges, affecting the "instantaneous decoherence" claims at resonance but not the suppression valleys between them; (3) the Appendix C Gibbs thermalization argument is logically incomplete — evenness of the decoherence rate Γ(Ω)=Γ(−Ω) does not imply the detailed-balance relation A(−Ω)/A(Ω)=e^{−βΩ} needed to fix a Gibbs asymptotic state.
papers.title: Cavity-controlled Inhibition of Decoherence in Accelerated Quantum Detectors

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper attempts to show how a recently proposed singularity-free black hole "rings" when disturbed by a massive scalar field, using two standard numerical techniques to map out the ringing frequency and decay rate. The review board found the core qualitative trends plausible and internally consistent, but the paper applies an established recipe to a single new geometry without introducing new methods or phenomena. The most important caveat is that the exotic matter holding the black hole together—a so-called phantom scalar field—has known theoretical instabilities that the paper does not address, so the physical significance of the results remains uncertain.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper applies high-order WKB+Padé (up to 16th order) and Gundlach-Price-Pullin time-domain integration with Prony extraction to compute massive scalar quasinormal modes of the Parvez-Shankaranarayanan phantom-DBI regular black hole. The qualitative trends—increasing μ raises Re(ω) and reduces |Im(ω)|; increasing regularity parameter a lowers both—are consistent with the established massive-QNM literature and are internally coherent between the two methods for representative cases. The quasiresonance threshold μ_c is an extrapolated estimate from quadratic fitting of WKB data in the regime where WKB accuracy degrades, acknowledged by the authors as requiring Leaver confirmation. A concrete textual inconsistency (Fig. 6 label: narrative says l=0, caption says l=1 referencing Table II) is unresolved. The phantom background's stability against ghost/gradient instabilities is not analyzed. All five numerical tables were absent from the HTML source used for review, preventing direct verification of the central quantitative results.
papers.title: Massive scalar quasinormal modes of an asymptotically flat regular black hole supported by a phantom Dirac--Born--Infeld field

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes that dark matter might carry an intrinsic spin property—arising from a generalized gravity framework called metric-affine gravity—that causes its effective behavior to vary over cosmic history, even though the dark matter itself has no intrinsic pressure. The review board found the underlying theoretical machinery sound and grounded in the authors' own prior published work, but the specific dark matter model is a phenomenological recipe without a deeper microphysical justification, and the claimed connection to recent DESI survey anomalies is only qualitative. For a 4-page conference proceedings paper, this is a plausible and timely preliminary proposal; a full evaluation must await the promised quantitative comparison with data.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper extends metric-affine (Palatini-type) flat FLRW cosmology to multi-component fluids, adding a pressureless dark dust component that carries spin hypermomentum with σ scaling as the square root of its density (so σ² ∝ ρ_DM). This scaling, fed into the effective hypermomentum density and pressure terms inherited from Andrei et al. PRD 111 (2025), generates a redshift-dependent effective barotropic index w_eff(z) during matter domination without any intrinsic pressure. The paper argues this mechanism may qualitatively match DESI DR2 hints of dynamical dark-sector behavior. Across two review cycles, three recurring technical concerns survived cross-examination: (1) the projective symmetry of the metric-affine Einstein-Hilbert action makes the Palatini connection equation degenerate unless a gauge or hypermomentum trace constraint is imposed—the paper states such a gauge only for the zero-hypermomentum case; (2) the spin-density closure ansatz is phenomenological, with no Lagrangian realization, ghost-freedom check, or Noether identity consistency; and (3) the DESI DR2 connection is entirely qualitative, with no likelihood analysis or w_eff(z) plot provided. All three reviewers independently recommended "routine" in both cycles.
papers.title: Friedmann cosmology with fluids and hyperfluids

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes that the 2025 LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA detection of a black-hole merger (GW250114) — which confirmed that total black-hole horizon area grows during mergers — can be used to sharply restrict the design of quantum gravity theories if that growth law is treated as exact. The review board found the core idea timely and non-trivial, as constraining quantum-scale theories using macroscopic gravitational-wave data is a genuinely novel diagnostic. However, several technical steps are omitted or incomplete in the four-page format, and the most fundamental concern is that the observational input itself relies on assuming Einstein gravity to infer horizon areas, creating a circularity when those areas are used to constrain non-Einstein theories. The paper is best read as a provocative no-go argument that requires further expert verification before its conclusions can be treated as established.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper argues that postulating exact Hawking area monotonicity in Stelle gravity and nonlocal/fractional quantum gravity (NLQG/FQG) forces: (i) vanishing action coefficients β₁ and β₂ for R² and (Riemann)² terms when non-Ricci-flat black hole solutions exist; (ii) absence of extra real poles in the FQG graviton propagator; and (iii) positivity of the inverse nonlocal operator's spectral representation. The Ricci-flat compatibility result is the cleanest claim, following directly from the fact that Schwarzschild/Kerr are exact solutions when β₂=0. The non-Ricci-flat constraint is derived from a low-curvature focusing condition (Eq. 10) in which curvature-squared terms are dropped — a sufficient but not necessary condition. The covariant Wald entropy extension to fractional form factors uses horizon geometric identities (e.g., f(□)R|_bifurcation = f(0)R|_bifurcation) that are asserted without proof for infinite-derivative operators. The FQG positivity condition is explicitly deferred to future work. The conclusion that Wald entropy monotonically tracks dynamical event-horizon area is stated without establishing the equivalence between stationary Noether-charge entropy and dynamical second-law entropy in this modified-gravity context.
papers.title: Hawking area law in quantum gravity

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper computes the characteristic "ringdown" oscillation frequencies of three types of test fields (scalar, electromagnetic, and fermionic) around a hypothetical singularity-free black hole built from an exotic scalar matter field. The review board found the calculation competently executed within an active niche, but incremental: the same toolkit has been applied to many similar backgrounds. The most important caveat is that the exotic "phantom" matter smoothing the singularity is susceptible to ghost-type instabilities, and the paper does not check whether the background itself is stable before computing oscillation modes on top of it. A second unresolved gap is that the scalar perturbation equation used may not correctly account for the coupling between the probe field and the scalar matter that sources the black hole.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper applies Padé-improved WKB (16th and 14th order) with two time-domain Prony spot-checks to compute fundamental quasinormal frequencies of massless scalar, electromagnetic, and Dirac test fields on the Parvez-Shankaranarayanan phantom-DBI regular black hole. The paper claims increasing the regularity parameter β monotonically shifts all fundamental QNFs toward the origin of the complex plane with spectral changes larger than internal WKB truncation estimates. Cross-review over two cycles identified three gaps that survived adversarial scrutiny: (1) the massless Klein-Gordon scalar is never distinguished from the background phantom DBI hair field, leaving the decoupling assumption unjustified for a hairy spacetime; (2) no stability analysis is performed for the phantom branch, so it is unclear whether damped modes correspond to physically observable ringdown; (3) the primary numerical uncertainty estimate — agreement between WKB16 and WKB14 Padé approximants — is a weak internal consistency check that can mask shared systematic bias, and time-domain validation covers only two of many tabulated modes. A further concrete flaw is that Table 4 includes electromagnetic overtone modes (n=3, ℓ=2) that fall outside the paper's own stated WKB reliability condition ℓ≥n. The "no peak found" entries in the scalar table remain ambiguous due to HTML rendering failure and require PDF-level spotcheck.
papers.title: Scalar, electromagnetic, and Dirac perturbations of a regular black hole supported by primordial dark matter

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes a new gravitational wave detector design—called a Pyramid interferometer—that could directly detect the "handedness" (chirality) of cosmological gravitational waves, a signature of parity-violating physics in the early universe that no current or planned detector can cleanly access. The review board found the geometric concept genuinely novel and potentially important for next-generation detector planning. However, the board is divided on whether the central mathematical claim—that the proposed geometry automatically isolates the chiral signal from the unpolarized background—actually holds for the described configuration. That dispute, along with an unverified assumption that a 1-km above-ground tower would achieve underground-level noise performance, must be resolved before the paper's sensitivity forecasts can be trusted.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper argues that adding one or two non-coplanar arms to the triangular Einstein Telescope layout creates a detector pair whose overlap reduction functions satisfy γ_I = 0 and γ_V ≠ 0 simultaneously—yielding exact geometric separation of the Stokes-I intensity and Stokes-V circular-polarization channels. The dispute among reviewers centers on whether the specific arm geometry of Eq. (13) actually achieves this: Gemini's tensor calculation finds the detector tensors are heavily aligned for any small-tilt pyramid, giving large γ_I, while Codex argues (after accessing the PDF) that the leading-order detector-tensor contraction does cancel for those specific arm vectors. If γ_I ≠ 0, the SNR formula (Eq. 20) omits the noise penalty of subtracting a much larger Stokes-I background, potentially invalidating Fig. 3. An independent concern raised by Claude is that γ_V is suppressed by O(h/L) for the underground Minimal Pyramid (h/L ~ 1–3%), a fundamental trade-off the paper does not discuss. The Tower-Extended variant's noise analysis is acknowledged by all reviewers to be insufficient, relying on the underground ET noise budget without quantifying tower-specific noise sources.
papers.title: Pyramid Interferometers: Direct Access to Cosmological Gravitational Wave Chirality

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes a new dark energy model called LTIT (Late-Transition Interacting Thawer) aimed at explaining hints from the DESI survey that dark energy may be evolving. The core idea—using a smooth "switch" in a scalar field to activate dark-energy interactions only at late cosmic times—is physically well-motivated and the background-level design logic is clear. The review board found the basic mechanism plausible but identified technical gaps, especially in how the model handles small-scale structure growth and perturbation equations, that prevent its strongest numerical predictions from being trusted without further work. The paper is best read as a theoretical framework that requires a full computer implementation before it can be compared to data.
papers.technical_takeaway: LTIT is a field-dependent coupled quintessence model in which a canonical scalar field conformally couples to CDM through a tanh step in field space. The exact CDM scaling identity (rho_c ∝ a^{-3}/C(phi)) and the transfer-driven effective phantom mechanism are well-supported exact results. Cross-review identified three major unresolved issues: (1) the synchronous-gauge perturbation system is formally under-determined after coupling activation because no replacement residual gauge condition is given for the abandoned theta_c=0 prescription; (2) the quasi-static growth approximation used for all benchmark growth curves likely omits the scalar effective-mass denominator and beta_phi coupling-rate terms that are essential for variable-coupling models, making the headline growth-response numbers unreliable in sign or magnitude; (3) the large negative beta_phi near the sharp tanh transition creates an unanalyzed tachyonic instability risk in the scalar perturbation sector. Together these issues mean the paper cannot yet be regarded as "solver-ready" despite that claim.
papers.title: Late-Transition Interacting Thawer Dark Energy: Physics and Validation

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper asks whether a recently proposed singularity-free ("Hayward-like") black hole would bend light differently from an ordinary black hole, and whether future telescopes could detect the difference. The review board found the calculations to be technically competent but methodologically routine. The most important caveat: the predicted differences are at the level of 30–50 nanoarcseconds — far below the reach of any current instrument — and the analysis assumes non-rotating black holes, while the two real-world targets (Sgr A* and M87*) are both known to spin.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper applies the Gauss-Bonnet theorem (GBT) for weak-field deflection and Bozza's Strong Deflection Limit (SDL) to the Hayward-like metric (Calza:2025mrt). The paper claims: (i) the shadow size θ_∞ is exactly Schwarzschild-degenerate for all ℓ, traced to a photon-sphere condition that is Schwarzschild-like when written in areal-radius coordinates; (ii) the subleading SDL observables — angular separation s, relative flux ratio r_m, and time delay Δt₁₂ — carry ℓ-dependent deviations; (iii) the weak-field deflection angle receives a positive O(M²ℓ²/b⁴) correction, contrasting with the negative deviation of the standard Hayward metric. The methodology is textbook for this subfield. The central algebraic result (ℓ-independence of u_c) is asserted without showing intermediate steps. A numerical inconsistency in Table 2 — the M87* angular separation s at ℓ=0 violates the source-independence identity s/θ_∞ = exp((b̄−2π)/ā) — was independently confirmed by all three reviewers across both cycles.
papers.title: Gravitational Lensing Signatures of Hayward-like Black Holes

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper tests a modified gravity theory — called nonlocal gravity — by studying how its predicted black holes bend light, comparing the results to measurements from the GRAVITY telescope and the Event Horizon Telescope. The review board found the lensing mathematics broadly sound but identified two serious problems in the statistical analysis: prior theoretical bounds were fed into the analysis as though they were real observational data, and a key gravitational measurement was applied in a way that implicitly assumes a result the paper never derives. The qualitative conclusion — that these nonlocal black holes are broadly consistent with current observations — is plausible, but the specific confidence levels quoted should not be taken at face value until these issues are addressed.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper derives WDL and SDL gravitational lensing observables for DD black holes in the revised Deser-Woodard nonlocal gravity model, correctly applying the Bozza (2002) SDL formalism and standard WDL series expansion. Two structural problems in the joint Fisher-matrix analysis were independently confirmed by multiple reviewers: (1) QNM-derived perturbativity tolerance intervals from a companion paper are entered as independent Gaussian likelihood terms on equal footing with EHT and GRAVITY measurements — a statistically unjustified conflation of theoretical range-of-validity criteria with observational data; (2) the GRAVITY S2 Schwarzschild-precession bound, which constrains the PPN combination (2+2γ−β)/3, is applied as a direct Gaussian prior on γ alone without computing β for the DD metric. Additional unresolved issues include the absence of a WDL deflection formula for 1 < k < 2 despite the final posterior extending below k = 2, and spin-related EHT systematics that are acknowledged but not propagated into the error budget.
papers.title: Observational constraints on nonlocal black holes via gravitational lensing

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper tries to show that the geometry deep inside a black hole—specifically near its singularity—changes character when the black hole undergoes a thermodynamic phase transition, and can serve as a novel diagnostic for that transition. The review board found the core numerical observation (two branches of the transition produce qualitatively different interior behavior) plausible and interesting. However, the paper's boldest claims—that this constitutes an "independent" probe and a "first identification"—do not survive scrutiny: earlier published work appears to have already studied singularity geometry across phase transitions, and the interior is mathematically fixed by exterior data rather than being truly independent. The most important caveat is an internally inconsistent definition of the paper's main new concept, the "Kasner crossover line."
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper numerically integrates the interior equations of motion for scalarized AdS black holes (charged scalar with a quartic nonlinear coupling that induces a first-order transition) from the horizon to the singularity, extracting the Kasner exponent p_t that characterizes near-singularity geometry. The paper claims: (1) p_t is oscillatory in temperature on the high-T scalarized branch and smooth on the low-T branch, converging near the critical point; (2) in the supercritical region a feature in p_t(T) defines a "Kasner crossover line" independent of Widom and Frenkel lines. The core oscillatory-vs-smooth contrast survived cross-review as a plausible numerical result, supported by the expected difference in Josephson oscillation counts between branches. Three objections were sustained across both review cycles: the "first identification" priority claim is contradicted by Liu-Lyu-Raju JHEP 2021 (Ref [22]) and the authors' own Ref [33]; the "Kasner crossover line" is defined inconsistently as both a smooth extremum and a non-analytic cusp, which are physically distinct (the latter would signal an interior dynamical phase transition, not a crossover); and the "independent probe" framing is conceptually overblown because the interior is deterministically fixed by horizon data via ODE integration.
papers.title: Interior geometry of black holes as a probe of first-order phase transition

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper applies standard black-hole physics tools to a specific theoretical modification of gravity called "nonlocal gravity," asking how this modification would alter the quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) signals observed in X-ray light from matter spiraling into black holes. The review board found the methodology internally consistent and the paper competently executed, but assessed it as a routine template calculation applied to one more theory variant without new physical insight or comparison to actual observational data. The most important caveat is that the analysis assumes a non-spinning black hole, while real astrophysical QPO sources host rapidly spinning black holes — a mismatch that substantially limits the physical applicability of the derived constraints.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper derives the geodesic and epicyclic frequency structure for the Nicolini-type static nonlocal gravity (NLG) black hole (parameter range α/M ≤ 0.452 per the raw HTML), demonstrates ν_K = ν_θ from spherical symmetry (reducing 11 QPO models to 7 effective models), and imposes the empirical 3:2 resonance condition to find the upper QPO frequency increasing with α, a TOV-limited upper frequency of roughly 1450 Hz, and a rough mass bound of order 43.6 M⊙. The methodology is standard for this class of phenomenological papers; the non-rotating approximation and absence of source-specific data fitting substantially limit the physical applicability of the derived constraints. A discrepancy between reviewer-recovered numerical values (gemini: ~30.6 M⊙ vs. codex cycle 2: ~43.6 M⊙) requires PDF verification.
papers.title: The properties and predictions of quasi-periodic oscillations around a black hole in nonlocal gravity

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper argues that a single mathematical function — derived from a classical "membrane" model of black hole horizons — can identify all the important geometric surfaces of a Kerr-Newman black hole at once: event horizons, ergospheres, the ring singularity, and asymptotic infinity each appear as a different algebraic feature (zero, pole, or divergence) of the same formula. The review board found the core algebraic observation plausible for a generic rotating black hole, but the headline claim is overstated: the encoding breaks down entirely in the non-rotating (Schwarzschild) limit, and the function's zeros mark more surfaces than just the horizons. The secondary analogy to a van der Waals fluid equation is a formal coincidence with no predictive content.
papers.technical_takeaway: For generic subextremal rotating off-axis Kerr-Newman (a≠0, a²+Q²<M², θ≠0,π), the analytically continued membrane pressure Π is argued to place Δ in its numerator (yielding horizon zeros) and (Δ−a²sin²θ) in its denominator (yielding ergosurface poles). However, three durable issues survive cross-review: (1) in the static limit a→0 and on the rotation axis, these factors coincide and cancel exactly, eliminating the claimed horizon zeros — so the unified detector fails precisely in the Schwarzschild case the paper illustrates in Fig. 2; (2) the numerator also contains a quadratic whose roots are the "balance surfaces" ρ±, making the actual zero set {r+, r−, ρ+, ρ−} rather than the two horizons alone, contrary to the abstract and conclusion; (3) the construction is foliation-adapted to Boyer-Lindquist r and is not established as a coordinate-invariant geometric detector comparable to curvature or Cartan invariants. The claimed uniform Σ⁻² divergence at the ring singularity is also path-dependent. Gemini's cycle-1 "fatal" claims (that Eq. (14) placed √Δ in the denominator and dropped a B'/B term) were fully retracted in cycle 2 as hallucinated due to HTML rendering preventing equation access.
papers.title: Geometrically Significant Surfaces of Black Holes from a Single Scalar

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper studies how a heavy quantum field oscillates when perturbed near a charged black hole that includes an extra "dilaton" scalar field motivated by string theory. The review board found the core trend — that heavier fields produce longer-lived oscillations, and that the dilaton coupling measurably shifts the spectrum — to be plausible and incrementally useful for gravitational-wave physics. However, the strongest conclusion, that oscillations can become arbitrarily long-lived at a precise critical field mass, is inferred by extrapolating beyond the regime where the computational method is reliable, without independent verification from a different approach. The paper would need that independent check before its boldest claims can be accepted.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper applies high-order WKB–Padé (orders 14 and 16) and Gundlach-Price-Pullin time-domain integration to massive scalar quasinormal modes of Einstein-Maxwell-dilaton (EMD) black holes for dilaton couplings α = 0, 1, √3. The well-validated result is that dilaton-induced spectral shifts dominate the WKB numerical uncertainty by roughly two orders of magnitude (~35–45% shift vs. ~0.1–0.5% order-difference), and that damping suppression with increasing scalar mass μ is clear in the l=1 sector, with sub-percent WKB–time-domain agreement (0.16% and 0.45% in complex-frequency norm). The contested result is the claim of quasi-resonances (Im(ω) → 0 at critical μ): this follows from extrapolated cubic splines beyond the demonstrably reliable WKB regime and not from a convergent near-axis method. Persistent cross-review concerns: (a) massive-field boundary conditions near the scalar mass threshold ω ≈ μ are not stated precisely enough to distinguish outgoing QNMs from quasi-bound states; (b) a claimed purely real frequency in the l=0, α=√3 sector lacks time-domain verification and is potentially a WKB breakdown artifact; (c) all time-domain benchmarks cover only the l=1 sector, leaving the l=0 sector, where the most prominent quasi-resonance claims appear, without independent validation.
papers.title: Quasi-resonances in the vicinity of Einstein-Maxwell-dilaton black hole

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper aims to show that three recently proposed ways of classifying black holes by their thermodynamic behavior — one based on the shape of their temperature curve, one using topological invariants, and one using complex-number extensions of thermodynamic quantities — all reduce to a single operation: counting the humps and valleys on the temperature curve. The review board found the synthesis idea useful and the core dictionary between local and global schemes well-grounded, but raised concerns about the rigor of the complex-analysis dictionary and identified at least one example (Schwarzschild-anti-de-Sitter) that appears to be misclassified. The paper is best read as a clarifying organizational contribution to a technical subfield rather than a new theorem, and its universality claims are narrower than stated.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper argues that the number of non-degenerate extrema of the Hawking temperature T(r_h) simultaneously determines: (i) the local geometric classification (Zhang et al. 2025a), (ii) the topological winding-number order in the global scheme (Wei et al. 2022/2024), and (iii) the Riemann-surface foliation count of the complexified free energy (Xu et al. 2024). Dictionary 1 (local↔global) rests on sign(C) = sign(dT/dr_h) under the assumption dS/dr_h > 0, which is definitionally close to the Wei et al. winding-number assignment and is well-supported for standard Einstein-gravity black holes. Dictionary 2 (real↔complex) invokes the Argument Principle to equate the zero count of the complexified derivative g(z) with the maximum number of physical states; this direction is directionally sound but requires an unverified no-pole, no-off-axis-zero condition and a range-overlap condition (T branches must share a common temperature level) that the paper does not state explicitly. A concrete example issue: the Schwarzschild-AdS temperature T = (1 + 3r_h²/l²)/(4πr_h) always possesses a minimum at r_h = l/√3 for all l > 0, making the paper's A+ (monotonically increasing) classification in Table 2 mathematically unjustified without an explicit domain cutoff. The framework is also acknowledged to break down for black holes exhibiting topological phase transitions.
papers.title: Unifying topological, geometric, and complex classifications of black hole thermodynamics

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This 6-page paper proposes that a modified theory of gravity called GEVAG can fix a known inconsistency in quantum-corrected black-hole physics: instead of black holes stopping evaporation at a hot "leftover" state (a finite-temperature remnant), the paper argues that correctly accounting for a running gravitational coupling drives the temperature smoothly to zero. The review board found the mathematical sketch internally consistent but built on an assumption the theory does not derive — that the gravitational coupling can be extended off the black-hole horizon — and the key "remarkable cancellation" at the minimum mass turns out to be a standard geometric consequence of the type of metric the assumption generates, not an independent confirmation. The result is an interesting conditional model observation, but it falls well short of the robust resolution of the remnant problem that the title and conclusions imply.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper claims that extending the GEVAG horizon-defined coupling G_eff(A) to a near-horizon radial function G_eff(4πr²) adds a derivative correction δT to the Hawking temperature, so that the full temperature T = T_GUP + δT vanishes at the minimum mass for logarithmic entropy corrections. Cross-review established that: (1) the algebraic equivalence of T_old and T_GUP (Eqs. 7–8) is consistent under the author's parameter identification, after consulting the PDF; (2) the zero-temperature cancellation at minimum mass is the expected extremal-horizon condition — a degenerate horizon satisfies f′(r_h) = 0 by definition, so the result is not an independent validation of the off-shell assumption; (3) the metric ansatz with spatially varying G(r) is not shown to solve the GEVAG effective field equations; (4) the standard Hawking temperature formula T = f′(r_h)/4π may require re-derivation when G is spatially varying; and (5) the first law in a varying-G setting may carry additional Noether-charge terms not accounted for in the thermodynamic energy and Bekenstein constant derivations. The RG-scaling interpretation of the Bekenstein bound is explicitly loose and unconnected to an actual RG flow.
papers.title: Corrected Hawking Temperature and Final State of Black Hole Evaporation Under GEVAG Framework

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper claims that popular cosmological models using "unimodular gravity with diffusion" — proposed to explain why two methods of measuring the universe's expansion rate disagree (the H₀ tension) — violate the second law of thermodynamics. The review board found the paper's algebra internally consistent within its stated assumptions, but all three reviewers independently identified a serious unaddressed premise: the argument applies thermodynamic constraints only to the matter component while ignoring whether the dark-energy sector can gain entropy. The most important caveat is whether that premise is justified, which requires expert verification of the cited prior literature.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper argues that in the CDM diffusion class of Unimodular Gravity (UG), applying the Gibbs equation to pressureless matter gives T Ṡ_matter = −Q̇V, so the second law requires Q̇ ≤ 0. Because the H₀ tension resolution mechanism in the target models requires a growing Λ_eff (Q̇ > 0), the paper formalizes a no-go theorem ruling out the entire class. The algebra is correct within the stated framework, but the framework rests on a load-bearing assumption — that the dynamical Λ_eff/Q sector carries zero thermodynamic entropy — which is inherited from Ref. [14] without independent justification. All three reviewers rate this gap as at least major; its resolution depends on whether UG cosmological thermodynamics conventions assign zero entropy to the cosmological integration constant sector, and whether that convention survives the Landau et al. model whose physical mechanism explicitly invokes black hole degrees of freedom.
papers.title: Comment on Cosmological constraints on unimodular gravity models with diffusion (arXiv:2211.07424): thermodynamic inadmissibility of the H0 tension resolution mechanism

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This comment paper challenges a 2025 published calculation of quantum energy levels for Dirac particles in a rotating, magnetized curved spacetime, arguing the original results are incomplete because they omit the angular quantum number. The review board found the core concern physically motivated—standard physics does require two quantum numbers in this type of polar-coordinate problem—but found the critique substantially weakened by two issues: first, in magnetic-field systems, energy levels can legitimately be independent of the angular number (Landau degeneracy), and the commenter's own formulas confirm this for a parameter sector that matches the original paper's results; second, several of the claimed sign errors in the original paper may be differences between valid mathematical representations rather than mistakes. Readers should treat the paper's conclusions as unresolved rather than definitive corrections.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper claims to correct the energy spectra of Sahan et al. [arXiv:2409.18535] for Dirac fermions in the Gürses (2+1)D magnetized rotating spacetime, arguing the spectra must depend on both radial (n) and angular (m) quantum numbers. The commenter identifies four alleged errors: missing m-dependence, inverse metric sign errors, spin connection sign and commutator errors, and a factor-of-2 in the cyclotron frequency. Cross-review raised two major unresolved objections: (1) for Landau-type problems in a magnetic field, m can label degenerate states without appearing explicitly in the energy—the commenter's own Eq. (37)-(38) shows an m-independent sector (m<0, ε=-1) that recovers Sahan et al., suggesting the original may be a valid restricted-sector result; (2) the normalizable hypergeometric ansatz requires E_k + m_e ω_c > 0, a condition not propagated to the final spectrum. The inverse metric sign correction—the strongest concrete claim—is disputed in cycle 2, with one reviewer arguing it rests on a metric convention mismatch (plus-sign vs. minus-sign off-diagonal term). The thermodynamic incorrectness claim is qualitative only, with no corrected partition function provided.
papers.title: Comment on "Quantum phase transitions of Dirac particles in a magnetized rotating curved background: Interplay of geometry, magnetization, and thermodynamics"

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper is a pedagogical walkthrough of conformal time in expanding universes, deriving how freely moving particles travel and how spacetime curves in three standard cosmological eras (radiation, matter, and vacuum domination). The review board found the paper technically competent and correct in its core results, but unanimously noted it contains no original research—all results appear in standard graduate textbooks. The most important caveat for a student reader is that the simplified 1+1-dimensional model used throughout cannot be derived from Einstein's equations in two dimensions, a fact the paper does not clearly state at the point of use.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper correctly derives conformal-time–scale-factor relations (a∝η for radiation, a∝η² for matter, a∝1/|η| for de Sitter), geodesic first integrals from translation invariance and affine normalization, affine-parameter asymptotics (cubic/quadratic crossover in the radiation era; quintic/cubic in the matter era), and hyperbolic de Sitter timelike trajectories in the conformal coordinate plane. An unresolved dispute between reviewers concerns whether the radiation-era affine integral in Eq.(17) carries the correct powers of the proportionality constant A_r: two reviewers read the paper as implicitly normalizing A_r=1 (standard in pedagogical treatments), while one reviewer maintains the printed formulas are dimensionally wrong even under that reading. The claim that Eq.(43) is "genuinely general for any spatially flat conformal metric" is overstated: the derivation requires a spatially homogeneous conformal factor a(η) and fails for the spatially dependent f(η,x) treated in Appendix A.
papers.title: A Survey through Conformal Time

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper argues that a large class of quantum gravity theories cannot eliminate the singularity at the center of a collapsing star, no matter how the quantum corrections are modeled — as long as the corrections only modify the matter content while the gravitational law itself remains a polynomial formula. The review board found the central mathematical argument for a specific gravity framework (symmetric teleparallel, or f(Q) gravity) intriguing and partially supported, particularly the demonstration that certain supposedly "regular" black hole models still contain hidden singularities invisible to outside observers. However, the paper's headline claim of universality — that the result extends to all analytic gravitational theories — is substantially overstated: the proof does not carry over to f(R) gravity, and the broader FLRW setting used is much narrower than the paper implies. Readers should treat this as a useful but limited no-go result for one specific class of gravity models, not a definitive ruling for quantum gravity as a whole.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper derives an algebraic bounce obstruction in spatially flat FLRW f(Q) gravity: the modified Friedmann constraint J(Q) = ε_eff with J(Q) = Q f'(Q) − f(Q)/2 forces J(0) = 0 at a bounce (H=0, Q=6H²→0) for polynomial actions with f(0)=0, which is incompatible with ε_eff > 0. For the saturated de Sitter asymptotic scenario (a→0, t→∞), SM Appendix V provides a clean ε–δ argument showing the null geodesic affine parameter integral converges, establishing geodesic incompleteness. Cross-review identified three surviving major objections: (1) the claimed extension to f(R) gravity is provably wrong — in flat FLRW, R = 6(Ḣ + 2H²) ≠ 0 at a bounce because Ḣ > 0 there, so the algebraic obstruction does not exist; (2) the proof is restricted to spatially flat, homogeneous, pressureless dust collapse in f(Q), not the "most general effective-source scenario" asserted; (3) the Cauchy–Schwarz argument in SM Appendix II used to show non-metricity scalar finiteness implies Kretschmann scalar finiteness is mathematically invalid in Lorentzian signature. The junction condition derivation and the cosmological-constant bounce dismissal each carry additional moderate flaws identified in cycle 2.
papers.title: No-Go Theorem for Singularity Resolution

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes an analogy between the Klein Paradox of relativistic quantum mechanics — where a particle tunnels through an impossibly large potential barrier — and the mechanical breakdown of an elastic material under stress. The goal is to give condensed-matter physics students a more intuitive picture of an otherwise abstract quantum field theory result. The review board found the pedagogical framing coherent and appropriate for its target journal (European Journal of Physics). The main caveat is that the paper overstates several technical claims: results are not actually derived from a concrete elastic medium, and the central concept of "topological anchoring" is never given a precise mathematical definition.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper maps the Klein Paradox to dielectric breakdown of a hypothetical linear elastic continuum, identifying mass with a resonant cutoff frequency, the potential step with external stress, and antiparticles with defect modes possessing "inverted topological anchoring." All three reviewers across both cycles converged on four major concerns: (1) the elastic medium is under-specified — no Lagrangian, defect boundary conditions from first principles, or conserved stress-energy tensor is ever written down, so the claim that results are "derived from continuum mechanics" is overclaimed; (2) Appendix A's spinor emergence argument is structurally circular — it assumes a massive relativistic Klein-Gordon equation, imposes first-order covariance, and recovers the Clifford algebra, which is Dirac's own original construction rather than a consequence of elastic mechanics; (3) the "topological anchoring" or "inverted winding" concept is used as the framework's interpretive core but is never given a precise mathematical definition (no winding number, homotopy class, conserved topological current, or representation-independent invariant is provided; the gamma^0 eigenvalue used as a stand-in is representation-dependent); and (4) the claimed recovery of the Schwinger pair-production rate is dimensional analogy only — no WKB tunneling action is computed and no exponent is matched. The paper's explicit self-description of its novelty as purely pedagogical is accurate and honest; the group-velocity argument and the R > 1 current conservation are reproduced correctly at the conceptual level.
papers.title: Hydrodynamic Analog of the Klein Paradox: Vacuum Instability and Pair Production in a Linear Elastic Medium

papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes a new mathematical framework for quantum gravity phenomenology, arguing that a known effect called "relative locality" — where distant observers disagree about where events occur — can arise even without the curved momentum geometry that has traditionally been thought necessary. The review board found the central conceptual idea (keeping ordinary spacetime, rather than momentum-space, as the primary arena) genuinely interesting and structurally novel. However, reviewers identified significant technical gaps in the mathematical proofs, particularly the missing construction linking momentum-space curvature to the paper's central geometric object. The paper reads as a promising but incomplete research program, with key results deferred to future work.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper introduces a fibre-bundle framework where spacetime M (not momentum-space) serves as the base manifold, defines an observer-dependent spacetime S via equivalence classes of locally measured momentum sections, and encodes deformed PQG dynamics through an Ehresmann connection H_D. The curvature-independence claim for relative locality is argued by a single flat-space free-particle example with an ad hoc constant horizontal lift, not a general proof.
